The **2023 review of Westminster constituencies** was the most recent cycle of the process to redraw the [constituency map](/wiki/Constituencies_of_the_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constituencies of the Parliament of the United Kingdom") for the [House of Commons of the United Kingdom](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"). The new constituency boundaries were approved by the [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council_(United_Kingdom) "Privy Council (United Kingdom)") on 15 November 2023[[1]](#cite_note-1) and came into law on 29 November.[[2]](#cite_note-2) It is the first review of Westminster boundaries to be successfully implemented since [2010](/wiki/Fifth_periodic_review_of_Westminster_constituencies "Fifth periodic review of Westminster constituencies"). These constituencies were first contested at the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election").

## Legal basis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Legal basis")]

The process for periodic reviews of parliamentary constituencies in the United Kingdom is governed by the [Parliamentary Constituencies Act 1986](/wiki/Parliamentary_Constituencies_Act_1986 "Parliamentary Constituencies Act 1986"), as amended by the [Parliamentary Voting System and Constituencies Act 2011](/wiki/Parliamentary_Voting_System_and_Constituencies_Act_2011 "Parliamentary Voting System and Constituencies Act 2011") and subsequently by the [Parliamentary Constituencies Act 2020](/wiki/Parliamentary_Constituencies_Act_2020 "Parliamentary Constituencies Act 2020").[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

## Individual registration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Individual registration")]

The 2023 review was the successor to the [2018 periodic review of Westminster constituencies](/wiki/2018_periodic_review_of_Westminster_constituencies "2018 periodic review of Westminster constituencies"), which was abandoned after it failed to pass into law. After abandonment of several previous reviews since 2015, the 2023 review was set to be the first review based on electoral registers drawn up using Individual Electoral Registration, which Parliament approved from 2014–15.[[4]](#cite_note-CM8109-4) Because every routine canvass by local government reaches slightly fewer imminent attainers of the age of 18 than the previous system of household registration,[[4]](#cite_note-CM8109-4) the new system favours the [Conservatives](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"), according to a University of Manchester researcher in 2023.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Local election offices are funded to implement mitigating measures to minimise any such disproportionate impacts.[[4]](#cite_note-CM8109-4) The Command Papers were sponsored and ordered by the Deputy Prime Minister, [Nick Clegg](/wiki/Nick_Clegg "Nick Clegg"), a [Liberal Democrat](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)").

## Process

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Process")]

Under current legislation, the [four boundary commissions of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Boundary_commissions_(United_Kingdom) "Boundary commissions (United Kingdom)") were required to report on their next review of the boundaries of parliamentary constituencies before 1 July 2023.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) In order to meet this deadline, the commissions began their work on 5 January 2021. Following three rounds of public consultation, all four commissions submitted their final proposals to the [Speaker](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_House_of_Commons_(United_Kingdom) "Speaker of the House of Commons (United Kingdom)") of the House of Commons on 27 June 2023. The Speaker immediately laid these before Parliament and the reports were published on the respective commissions' websites the following day.[[6]](#cite_note-:19-6) The new boundaries were formally introduced into UK law on 15 November 2023 through The Parliamentary Constituencies Order 2023.[[7]](#cite_note-:21-7) (The original deadline of the end of October was missed by the government, which, according to the Act, must only happen in exceptional circumstances).

A description of the review process is detailed in [timeline of the 2023 periodic review of Westminster constituencies](/wiki/Timeline_of_the_2023_periodic_review_of_Westminster_constituencies "Timeline of the 2023 periodic review of Westminster constituencies").

## Size of constituencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Size of constituencies")]

The four [boundary commissions](/wiki/Boundary_commissions_(United_Kingdom) "Boundary commissions (United Kingdom)") launched their 2023 reviews on 5 January 2021,[[8]](#cite_note-:2-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:3-11) to coincide with the release by the [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics") (ONS) of electorate data from analysis of the [electoral registers](/wiki/Electoral_Register "Electoral Register") that had been published on 2 March 2020.[[12]](#cite_note-:7-12) The commissions jointly calculated the relevant electoral quota/range to be used for the 2023 review and the allocation of parliamentary constituencies between the four nations. The English commission further divided its allocation between the nine [regions of England](/wiki/Regions_of_England "Regions of England").

The electorate of the United Kingdom, comprising 650 constituencies, as determined by the ONS, was 47,558,398 on 2 March 2020. The electorate of the five protected constituencies – [Isle of Wight](/wiki/Isle_of_Wight_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Isle of Wight (UK Parliament constituency)") (two seats), [Na h-Eileanan an Iar](/wiki/Na_h-Eileanan_an_Iar_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Na h-Eileanan an Iar (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Orkney and Shetland](/wiki/Orkney_and_Shetland_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Orkney and Shetland (UK Parliament constituency)"), and [Ynys Môn](/wiki/Ynys_M%C3%B4n_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Ynys Môn (UK Parliament constituency)") – amounted to 220,132, leaving 47,338,266 to be distributed between the remaining 645 constituencies, which gave an electoral quota of 73,393. Each non-protected constituency must have an electorate which is within 5% of this quota, which gave a permitted range of 69,724 to 77,062.[[8]](#cite_note-:2-8) In Northern Ireland the legislation allows for a wider range, in certain prescribed circumstances, from 68,313 to 77,062.[[11]](#cite_note-:3-11)

## Final proposals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Final proposals")]

See also: [Timeline of the 2023 review of Westminster constituencies](/wiki/Timeline_of_the_2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies "Timeline of the 2023 review of Westminster constituencies")

All four commissions submitted their Final Recommendations Reports to the speaker of the House of Commons on 27 June 2023. These were immediately laid before Parliament and the reports were published on the respective commissions' websites on 28 June 2023.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-:1-15)[[16]](#cite_note-:5-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Wales0623-17)

### England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: England")]

The final recommendations for England resulted in only 55 of the existing 533 constituencies remaining completely unchanged.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

In Wales, 21 of the 32 seats were unaltered from the revised proposals; 10 had revised boundaries (of which five were renamed); and one had a name change only.[[17]](#cite_note-Wales0623-17) In Scotland boundary changes affected 18 seats, of which 12 were renamed. Only name changes affected a further six, leaving 33 unaltered. In Northern Ireland, minor boundary changes affected five seats, with no changes to the proposed names.

### Northern Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Northern Ireland")]

As the number of constituencies in Northern Ireland remains the same, changes were only necessary to bring some of the electorates within the permitted range and align boundaries with those of revised local government wards. [Belfast South](/wiki/Belfast_South_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Belfast South (UK Parliament constituency)") was enlarged into the countryside and renamed [Belfast South and Mid Down](/wiki/Belfast_South_and_Mid_Down "Belfast South and Mid Down"). [East Antrim](/wiki/East_Antrim_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "East Antrim (UK Parliament constituency)") was extended further west, while [Fermanagh and South Tyrone](/wiki/Fermanagh_and_South_Tyrone_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Fermanagh and South Tyrone (UK Parliament constituency)") was extended further east into County Armagh.[[15]](#cite_note-:1-15)

### Political impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Political impact")]

According to analysis carried out by electoral modelling consultancy [Electoral Calculus](/wiki/Electoral_Calculus "Electoral Calculus"), a total of 24 constituencies would disappear (i.e. be broken up and not form the larger part of any proposed seats), offset by 24 wholly new constituencies (proposed seats which do not contain the larger part of any pre-existing seat). If the 2019 general election was re-run under the boundaries in the final proposals, it was estimated that a further 15 seats would change hands. The overall effect would be a net gain of 11 seats for the Conservatives, a net loss of 6 for Labour, a loss of 3 for the Liberal Democrats and 2 for Plaid Cymru.[[32]](#cite_note-new-32) This was further analysed as follows:

| Party | New seats | Abolished seats | Seats changing hands | | Total |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Gain | Loss |
| [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") | +19 | −11 | +8 | −5 | **+11** |
| [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") | +5 | −10 | +4 | −5 | **−6** |
| [Liberal Democrat](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") | – | – | – | −3 | **−3** |
| [Scottish National](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") | – | −2 | +3 | −1 | **0** |
| [Plaid Cymru](/wiki/Plaid_Cymru "Plaid Cymru") | – | −1 | – | −1 | **−2** |
| **Total** | **+24** | **−24** | **+15** | **−15** | **–** |

In January 2024, professors [Colin Rallings](/wiki/Colin_Rallings "Colin Rallings") and [Michael Thrasher](/wiki/Michael_Thrasher "Michael Thrasher") published detailed estimates of what the result would have been had the new boundaries been in place at the previous general election. This analysis shows the Conservatives would have won seven additional seats in 2019, with Labour losing two, the Liberal Democrats three and Plaid Cymru two.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

## New and abolished constituencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: New and abolished constituencies")]

See also: [List of United Kingdom Parliament constituencies (2024–present) by region](/wiki/List_of_United_Kingdom_Parliament_constituencies_(2024%E2%80%93present)_by_region "List of United Kingdom Parliament constituencies (2024–present) by region")

In total, the review produced 211 newly named constituencies, with the same number of seat names no longer being used (comprising just under one third of the total of 650). These constituencies are listed alphabetically below. Note that a constituency name remaining the same or changing does not necessarily correlate with how much the seat's boundaries change, or whether the constituency would be seen as a "new constituency". For example, the pre-2023 [Burton](/wiki/Burton_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Burton (UK Parliament constituency)") changes name to become the post-2023 [Burton and Uttoxeter](/wiki/Burton_and_Uttoxeter "Burton and Uttoxeter"), but its boundaries remain identical; on the other hand, the constituency name [Newcastle upon Tyne North](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne_North "Newcastle upon Tyne North") remains the same after the review, but the majority of the post-review constituency covers different territory to the pre-review constituency, with only 43% of the pre-2023 Newcastle upon Tyne North remaining in the new seat after the review.

### Disappearing and newly created seats

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Disappearing and newly created seats")]

Most of the new seats listed above are the result of name changes to existing constituencies following boundary changes of varying degrees, including five where the boundaries are unchanged. An existing seat where no part forms the largest part of any new seat is considered to be "disappearing". Conversely, any new seat which does not contain the largest part of any existing seat is considered to be genuinely "newly created".

The table below lists the disappearing and newly created constituencies.[[32]](#cite_note-new-32)

## Linked seats

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Linked seats")]

An existing seat can be regarded as being linked to a newly named seat where part of the existing seat contributes the largest part of the newly named seat. There are a total of 187 linked constituencies, many of which involve significant changes resulting from the knock-on impact of new or abolished seats within review areas. However, some arise from the consultation process and involve only minor changes. The table below lists those constituencies with name changes, indicating the extent of the changes by reference to the proportion of the old constituency included in the new constituency, or the proportion of the new in the old, which ever is the lesser:

* None – name change only
* Minor – greater than 90%
* Moderate – between 75% and 90%
* Major – between 50% and 75%
* Wholesale – less than 50%

## Notional 2019 general election results

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Notional 2019 general election results")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/2019UKElectionNominalMap.svg/250px-2019UKElectionNominalMap.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2019UKElectionNominalMap.svg)

The notional results of the 2019 election, if they had taken place under the post-2024 boundaries.

Researchers [Michael Thrasher](/wiki/Michael_Thrasher "Michael Thrasher") and [Colin Rallings](/wiki/Colin_Rallings "Colin Rallings") have calculated the notional results of the [2019 general election](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "2019 United Kingdom general election") if it had taken place under the new constituency borders. In constituencies whose borders have changed, precise results under the new boundaries usually cannot be known as election results are not usually reported for subdivisions of constituencies. However, it is possible to estimate what the election results would have been by extrapolating from local election results for which more granular data is known.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Most news organisations, including the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"), use these notional results for statistical purposes such as when calculating the swing from the 2019 general election to the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election") by constituency.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

The "Change" column is an index used by Thrasher and Rallings to refer to how radically the borders of the constituency in question changed in the boundary review. "None" is no change in the borders (therefore the notional results are identical to the actual results), a small number denotes little change, and a larger number denotes more change.
